standard_description: Apply and extend previous understandings of multiplication to multiply a fraction or whole number by a fraction.
subject: Math

Task
1/4 mile Track
###IMAGE0###
1 lap = 1/4 mile
Part One
Mrs. Gray gave a homework assignment with a fraction problem:
Will ran $1 \frac23$ laps of a $\frac14$ mile track. How far, in miles, did Will run? Jenna and Steve worked together on solving the problem. Jenna said that Will ran about $\frac12$ mile because $1 \frac23 \times \frac14$ is equal to about $\frac12$. Steve answered that Will must have run more than $\frac12$ mile because when you multiply, the product is always larger than the factors and $\frac12$ is not larger than $1 \frac23$.
Solve the problem. How far, in miles, did Will run?
Is Jenna or Steve correct? Explain your reasoning using words, numbers, and/or pictures.
Part Two
Steve and Jenna continued to work on their homework. The next problems were:
$\frac13 \times 5 = $
$\frac12 \times 2 \frac23 =$
Steve said to Jenna, “Now I get it! When you multiply, the product is always bigger than one of the factors. In the first problem, $\frac13 \times 5$ equals $\frac53$ which is bigger than $\frac13$. In the second problem $\frac12 \times 2 \frac23$ equals $1 \frac13$ which is bigger than $\frac12$."
Is Steve’s reasoning correct? Does his rule that the product is always bigger than one of the factors always work?
Give at least two examples to prove that Steve is correct or incorrect.

Problem 1
Some of the problems below can be solved by multiplying
$$\tfrac{1}{8}\times\tfrac{2}{5}$$
, while others need a different operation. Select the ones that can be solved by multiplying these two numbers. For the remaining, tell what operation is appropriate. In all cases, solve the problem (if possible) and include appropriate units in the answer.
a.   Two-fifths of the students in Anya’s fifth-grade class are girls. One-eighth of the girls wear glasses. What fraction of Anya’s class consists of girls who wear glasses?
b.   A farm is in the shape of a rectangle
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of a mile long and
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
of a mile wide. What is the area of the farm?
c.   There is
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of the original whole pizza, what fraction of the original pizza is left over?
d.
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of kids in a class are boys. Of those boys,
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
have red hair. What fraction of the class is red-haired boys?
e.   Only
$$\tfrac{1}{20}$$
of the guests at the party wore both red and green. If
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of the guests wore red, what fraction of the guests who wore red also wore green?
f.   Alex was planting a garden. He planted
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
of the garden with potatoes and
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of the garden with lettuce. What fraction of the garden is planted with potatoes or lettuce?
g.   At the start of the trip, the gas tank on the car was
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
full. If the trip used
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of the remaining gas, what fraction of a tank of gas is left at the end of the trip?
h.   On Monday,
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of the students in Mr. Brown’s class were absent from school. The nurse told Mr. Brown that
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
of those students who were absent had the flu. What fraction of the absent students had the flu?
i.   Of the children at a daycare,
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
are boys and
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
of the boys are under 1 year old. How many boys at the daycare are under 1 year old?
j.   Jason lives
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$
of a mile from school. Grace lives
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
as far from school as Jason does. How far, in miles, does Grace live from school?

b.   Find the number of people in that section of the beach that are wearing both a hat and sunglasses.
c.   Find the number of people in that section of the beach that are wearing a hat but not wearing sunglasses.

Task
The 5th graders want to raise money for their overnight camping trip by selling cornbread during the school Chili Cook-Off contest. All the pans of cornbread are square.  A pan of cornbread costs \(\$12\). The customers can buy any fractional part of a pan of cornbread and pay that fraction of \(\$12\). For example, \(\frac{1}{2}\) of a full pan costs \(\frac{1}{2}\) of \(\$12\).
Mrs. Farmer buys cornbread from a pan that is \(\frac{1}{4}\) full.  She buys \(\frac{1}{3}\) of the remaining cornbread in the pan.
What fraction of the whole pan of cornbread does she buy?  Use objects and/or a diagram to show how much of the pan of cornbread she buys.
What does she pay for the cornbread she bought?  Use objects and/or a diagram to show how much pays.
The next customer is the school principal.  He buys cornbread from a different pan that is \(\frac{1}{2}\) full. He buys \(\frac{5}{6}\) of the remaining cornbread in the pan.
What fraction of the whole pan of cornbread does he buy?  Use a diagram to show how much of the pan of cornbread he  buys.
What does he pay for the cornbread he bought?  Use a diagram to show how much he will pay for his part of the pan.
What would be the cost of the cornbread the principal bought if the price of the entire pan changed to:
\(\$24\)
\(\$6\)
\(\$18\)
Use a diagram to explain each answer.

The purpose of this task is to provide students with a concrete experience they can
relate to fraction multiplication. Perhaps more importantly, the task also purposefully
relates length and locations of points on a number line, a common trouble spot for
students. This task is meant for instruction and would be a useful as part of an
introductory unit on fraction multiplication.
The Standards for Mathematical Practice focus on the nature of the learning
experiences by attending to the thinking processes and habits of mind that students
need to develop in order to attain a deep and flexible understanding of mathematics.
Certain tasks lend themselves to the demonstration of specific practices by students.
The practices that are observable during exploration of a task depend on how
instruction unfolds in the classroom. While it is possible that tasks may be connected to
several practices, only one practice connection will be discussed in depth. Possible
secondary practice connections may be discussed but not in the same degree of detail. 
This particular task engages students in Mathematical Practice Standard 5, Use
appropriate tools strategically. The students are directed to create a number line
marking 1/6, 2/6, 3/6, 4/6, and 5/6.  Then they carefully cut out a strip of paper of 5/6
length and fold it in half and then fold it in half again. The paper folding along with the
connection to the number line helps students visualize that when you divide a fraction
in half, it is the same as multiplying ½ by 5/6.  The same thinking corresponds to folding
the strip once again in half. Folding the paper in half twice is really the same as folding
it into fourths or multiplying 1/4 by 5/6.

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$3\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $3\dfrac12$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 3\dfrac{1}{2} \times 4$

$ = ~\dfrac72 \times 4$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{7\times 4}{2}$
$=\dfrac{ 7  ~\times \stackrel{2}{\cancel4}  }{  \underset{1}{\cancel{2}}} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{7 \times 2}{1}$
The product is $14$.

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$1\dfrac{2}{3} \times 6 $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac23$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 1\dfrac{2}{3} \times 6$

$ = ~\dfrac53 \times 6$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{5\times 6}{3}$
$=\dfrac{ 5  ~\times \stackrel{2}{\cancel6}  }{  \underset{1}{\cancel{3}}} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{5 \times 2}{1}$
The product is $10$.

items.question_content:
**Multiply. **

$3 \times 2\dfrac{2}{5}$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $2\dfrac2{5}$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 3 \times 2\dfrac{2}{5}$

$ = 3 \times \dfrac{12}{5} $  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{3\times 12}{5}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{36}{5}$ .

We can also write this as $7\dfrac15$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}=3\dfrac{3}{7} \times 2$
hints: First, let's rewrite $3\dfrac37$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 3\dfrac{3}{7} \times 2$

$ = ~\dfrac{24}7 \times 2$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{24\times 2}{7}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{48}{7}$ .

We can also write this as $6\dfrac67$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$ 5\times 1\dfrac{3}{4} ~=~ $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac3{4}$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 5 \times 1\dfrac{3}{4}$

$ = 5 \times \dfrac{7}{4} $  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{5\times 7}{4}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{35}{4}$ .

We can also write this as $8\dfrac34$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$1\dfrac{1}{3} \times 1\dfrac34 $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac13$ and $1\dfrac34$ as fractions. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 1\dfrac{1}{3} \times 1\dfrac34$

$ = ~\dfrac{4}3 \times \dfrac74$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{4\times 7}{3 \times4}$
$=\dfrac{ \stackrel{1}{\cancel{4}} \times~ 7  }{  3 \times\underset{1}{\cancel{4}}} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{1 \times 7}{3 \times 1}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{7}{3}$ .

We can also write this as $2\dfrac13$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$1\dfrac{2}{9} \times 1\dfrac45 $ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac29$ and $1\dfrac45$ as fractions. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 1\dfrac{2}{9} \times 1\dfrac45$

$ = ~\dfrac{11}9 \times \dfrac95$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{11\times 9}{9 \times5}$
$=\dfrac{ 11  ~\times \stackrel{1}{\cancel{9}}  }{  \underset{1}{\cancel{9}}\times 5} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{11 \times 1}{1 \times 5}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{11}{5}$ .

We can also write this as $2\dfrac15$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}=3\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3\dfrac12$
hints: First, let's rewrite $3\dfrac12$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 3\dfrac{1}{2} \times 3\dfrac12$

$ = ~\dfrac{7}2 \times \dfrac{7}2$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{7\times 7}{2 \times2}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{49}{4}$ .

We can also write this as $12\dfrac1{4}$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$2\dfrac{2}{3} \times 2\dfrac23 ~=~ $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $2\dfrac23$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 2\dfrac{2}{3} \times 2\dfrac23$

$ = ~\dfrac{8}3 \times \dfrac{8}3$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{8\times 8}{3 \times3}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{64}{9}$ .

We can also write this as $7\dfrac1{9}$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$2\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac45$

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $2\dfrac34$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 2\dfrac{3}{4} \times \dfrac45$

$ = ~\dfrac{11}4 \times \dfrac45$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{11\times 4}{4 \times5}$
$=\dfrac{ 11 ~ \times \stackrel{1}{\cancel4}  }{  \underset{1}{\cancel{4}}\times 5} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{11 \times 1}{1 \times 5}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{11}{5}$ .

We can also write this as $2\dfrac15$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$1\dfrac{1}{3} \times \dfrac67$ 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac13$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.

$\phantom{=} 1\dfrac{1}{3} \times \dfrac67$

$ = ~\dfrac{4}3 \times \dfrac67$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{4\times 6}{3 \times7}$
$=\dfrac{ 4  ~\times \stackrel{2}{\cancel6}  }{  \underset{1}{\cancel{3}}\times 7} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{4 \times 2}{1 \times 7}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{8}{7}$ .

We can also write this as $1\dfrac17$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

 [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}=\dfrac{6}{11} \times 1\dfrac16$
hints:
First, let's rewrite $1\dfrac1{6}$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.
$\phantom{=} \dfrac{6}{11} \times 1\dfrac16$

$ = \dfrac{6}{11} \times \dfrac{7}6$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{{6}\times 7}{{11} \times6}$
$=\dfrac{ \stackrel{1}{\cancel{6}}\times~ 7  }{  11 \times\underset{1}{\cancel{6}}} \qquad$ [[☃ explanation 3]]
$=\dfrac{1 \times 7}{11 \times 1}$
$=\dfrac{7}{11}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{7}{11}$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

 [[☃ numeric-input 2]] ${}=\dfrac{4}{5} \times 2\dfrac23$
hints:
First, let's rewrite $2\dfrac2{3}$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.
$\phantom{=} \dfrac{4}{5} \times 2\dfrac23$

$ = \dfrac{4}{5} \times \dfrac{8}3$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{{4}\times 8}{{5} \times3}$
$=\dfrac{32}{15}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{32}{15}$ .

We can also write this as $2\dfrac{2}{15}$ .

items.question_content:
**Multiply.**

$\dfrac{3}{11} \times 3\dfrac14 ~=~ $ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
First, let's rewrite $3\dfrac1{4}$ as a fraction. Then, we can multiply.
$\phantom{=} \dfrac{3}{11} \times 3\dfrac14$

$ = \dfrac{3}{11} \times \dfrac{13}4$  $\qquad$[[☃ explanation 1]]
$=\dfrac{{3}\times 13}{{11} \times4}$
$=\dfrac{39}{44}$

The product, in lowest terms, is $\dfrac{39}{44}$ .

items.question_content:
Priya spent $1\dfrac12$ days hiking. She was lost $\dfrac56$ of the time that she was hiking. 

**What fraction of a day was Priya lost?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] days
hints:
Priya was lost $\blueD{\dfrac56}$ of $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueD{\dfrac56}$ of $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ is another way of saying $\blueD{\dfrac56} \times \purpleD{1\dfrac12}$.

Let's change $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD2+\purpleD1}\purpleD2\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac32}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac56}\text{ of }\purpleD{1\dfrac12} &= \blueD{\dfrac56} \times \purpleD{\dfrac32}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD5 \times \purpleD3}{\blueD6 \times \purpleD2}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD5\times{}\stackrel{\purpleD1}{\cancel{\purpleD3}}  }{ \underset{\blueD2}{\cancel{\blueD6}}\times\purpleD2 } \\\\
&= \dfrac5{4}\\\\
&= 1\dfrac1{4}
\end{align}$
Priya was lost $1\dfrac1{4}$ days.

hints:

$\blueD{\dfrac34}$ of $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ is another way of saying $\blueD{\dfrac34}\times\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$.

Let's change $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD2+\purpleD1}\purpleD2\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac32}
\end{align}$

Natasha was asleep on the beach $\dfrac98$ hours.

We can also write this as $1\dfrac18$ hours.

A punch bowl had $2\dfrac12$ liters of juice in it.  Liam drank $\dfrac13$ of the juice in the bowl.

hints:

Let's change $\purpleD{2\dfrac12}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{2\dfrac12} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD2+\purpleD2+\purpleD1}\purpleD2\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac52}
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
At the candy store, Sophie filled a bag with $2\dfrac23$ kilograms of candy. $\dfrac14$ of the weight of the candy was from chocolate covered pretzels. 

 **How much did the chocolate covered pretzels in Sophie's bag weigh?**

[[☃ input-number 1]] of a kilogram
hints:
The chocolate covered pretzels weighed $\blueD{\dfrac14}$ of $\purpleD{2\dfrac23}$ kilograms.

[[☃ image 1]]
$\blueD{\dfrac14}$ of $\purpleD{2\dfrac23}$ is another way of saying $\blueD{\dfrac14} \times \purpleD{2\dfrac23}$.

Let's change $\purpleD{2\dfrac23}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{2\dfrac23} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac23}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac33} +\purpleD{\dfrac33} +\purpleD{\dfrac23}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD3+\purpleD3+\purpleD2}\purpleD3\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac83}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac14}\text{ of }\purpleD{2\dfrac23}&= \blueD{\dfrac14} \times \purpleD{\dfrac83}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD1 \times \purpleD8}{\blueD4 \times \purpleD3}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD1\times{}\stackrel{\purpleD2}{\cancel{\purpleD8}}  }{ \underset{\blueD1}{\cancel{\blueD4}}\times\purpleD3 } \\\\
&= \dfrac23
\end{align}$
The chocolate covered pretzels in Sophie's bag of candy weighed $\dfrac23$ of a kilogram.

In January, she used $\dfrac35$ of the amount of oil that she used in February.

**Fill in the amount of oil that Jackie used in January in the table below.**

Month | Liters of oil used
:-: | :-: 
January  | [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
February | $\dfrac23$
March | $1\dfrac12$
hints:
Jackie used $\purpleD{\dfrac35}$ of $\blueD{\text{February's amount}}$ in January. 

She used $\blueD{\dfrac23 \text{ of a liter}}$ in February.


[[☃ image 1]]
$\purpleD{\dfrac35}$ of $\blueD{\dfrac23}$ is another way of saying $\purpleD{\dfrac35}\times\blueD{\dfrac23}$.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{\dfrac35}\text{ of }\blueD{\dfrac23} &= \purpleD{\dfrac35} \times \blueD{\dfrac23}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD3\times\blueD2}{\purpleD5 \times \blueD3}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\stackrel{\purpleD1}{\cancel{\purpleD3}} \times ~\blueD2}{~\purpleD5 \times \underset{\blueD1}{\cancel{\blueD3}}}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD1\times\blueD2}{\purpleD5 \times \blueD1}\\\\
&= \dfrac{2}{5}
\end{align}$
Jackie used $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of a liter of oil in January.

items.question_content:
The dolphins at the San Diego zoo eat $3\dfrac18$ buckets of fish each day. The killer whales eat $2\dfrac35$ times as much fish as the dolphins.

**How many buckets of fish do the killer whales eat each day?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] buckets
hints:
The killer whales eat $2\dfrac35$ times the amount of the dolphins' $3\dfrac18$ buckets.

[[☃ image 1]]

To find the amount of buckets the killer whales eat, we multiply: 

$\purpleD{2\dfrac35}\times\blueD{3\dfrac18}$

Let's change $\purpleD{2\dfrac35}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{2\dfrac35} &= \purpleD{2} +\purpleD{\dfrac35}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac55} + \purpleD{\dfrac55} +\purpleD{\dfrac35}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD5 +\purpleD5 +\purpleD3}\purpleD5\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac{13}{5}}
\end{align}$

Now, let's change $\blueD{3\dfrac18}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{3\dfrac18} &= \blueD{3} +\blueD{\dfrac18}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac88} + \blueD{\dfrac88} + \blueD{\dfrac88} +\blueD{\dfrac18}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD8+\blueD8+\blueD8+\blueD1}\blueD8\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{25}8}
\end{align}$

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{2\dfrac{3}5} \times \blueD{3\dfrac{1}8} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{13}5} \times \blueD{\dfrac{25}8} \\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{13}\times\blueD{25}}{\purpleD5 \times \blueD8}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{13}\times{}\stackrel{\blueD5}{\cancel{\blueD{25}}}  }{ \underset{\purpleD1}{\cancel{\purpleD5}}\times\blueD8 } \\\\
&= \dfrac{65}{8}\\\\
&=8\dfrac{1}{8}
\end{align}$
The killer whales eat $8\dfrac{1}{8}$ buckets of fish each day.

**How many hours did Carlos spend practicing piano?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hours
hints:
Carlos spent $1\dfrac14$ times $1\dfrac14$ hours practicing piano.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's change ${1\dfrac14}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
{1\dfrac14} &= {1} +{\dfrac14}\\\\
&= {\dfrac44} +{\dfrac14}\\\\
&= \dfrac{4+1}4\\\\
&={\dfrac{5}{4}}
\end{align}$
Carlos spent $1\dfrac9{16}$ hours practicing piano.

items.question_content:
Junior's brother is $1\dfrac12$ meters tall.  Junior is $1\dfrac15$ of his brother's height.  

**How tall is Junior?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  meters
hints:
Junior is $1\dfrac15$ times $1\dfrac12$ meters tall.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's change $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD2+\purpleD1}\purpleD2\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac32}
\end{align}$
Now, let's change $\blueD{1\dfrac15}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{1\dfrac15} &= \blueD{1} +\blueD{\dfrac15}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac55}  +\blueD{\dfrac15}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD5+\blueD1}\blueD5\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{6}5}
\end{align}$
$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} \times \blueD{1\dfrac{1}5} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{3}2} \times \blueD{\dfrac{6}5} \\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times\blueD{6}}{\purpleD2 \times \blueD5}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times{}\stackrel{\blueD3}{\cancel{\blueD{6}}}  }{ \underset{\purpleD1}{\cancel{\purpleD2}}\times\blueD5 } \\\\
&= \dfrac{9}{5}\\\\
&=1\dfrac{4}{5}
\end{align}$
Junior is $1\dfrac{4}{5}$ meters tall.

items.question_content:
$4$ unit squares each have $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of their area shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $4$?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] square units
hints:
There are $4$ unit squares. $\blueE{\dfrac45}$ of each square is shaded.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}$ to find the total shaded area.
$\begin{align}
&\quad~~4 \times \blueE{\dfrac45}\\\\
&=\dfrac{4 \times \blueE4}{\blueE5}\\\\
&=\dfrac{16}{5}
\end{align}$

The total shaded area is  $\dfrac{16}{5}$ square units.

This can also be written as $3\dfrac15$ square units.

options.alt: A row of four identical rectangles. Each rectangle is divided into five equal parts. Each part of each rectangle is labeled one-fifth. The first four parts of each rectangle are shaded. The last part of each rectangle is not shaded.

hints: We see a set of $10$ triangles. The set is split into $5$ equal sections, or *fifths*.

$\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10$ is another way of saying $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$.

To find $\dfrac{1}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $1$ fifth. How many triangles are shaded?

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $2$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{2}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $2$ fifths.


[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $4$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{3}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $3$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $6$ triangles.

To find $\dfrac{4}{5}$ of $10$, we can shade $4$ fifths.

[[☃ image 1]]

We shaded $8$ triangles.

These are the products.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{1}{5} \times 10 &= 2\\\\
\dfrac{2}{5} \times 10 &= 4\\\\
\dfrac{3}{5} \times 10 &= 6\\\\
\dfrac{4}{5} \times 10 &= 8
\end{align}$

items.question_content:
You are planning a movie marathon.  Each movie lasts $\dfrac32$ hours.  You want to watch $4$ movies nonstop. 

**How many hours will your movie marathon last?**  

[[☃ input-number 1]]  hours
hints:
Each movie lasts $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ hours.


[[☃ image 1]]

You want to watch $\blueD{4}$ movies nonstop.


[[☃ image 1]]

You can multiply $\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD{\dfrac32}$ to figure out how long your movie marathon will last.

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times} \maroonD{\dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\maroonD3} {\maroonD2}\\\\
&={\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=6
\end{align}$
Your movie marathon will last $6$ hours.

items.question_content:
Sandy bought $6$ medium cartons of blueberries. Each medium carton held $\dfrac3{4}$ liter of blueberries. 

Lindsay bought $2$ large cartons of blueberries. Each large carton held $\dfrac9{4}$ liters of blueberries. 

**Who bought more liters of blueberries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Sandy

Sandy bought $6$ cartons with $\dfrac34$ liter of blueberries in each carton.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{6 \times \dfrac34}$ to figure out how many liters of blueberries Sandy bought:

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{6 \times \dfrac34}\\\\
=&\dfrac{\blueD{6 \times}\blueD3} {\blueD4}\\\\
=&\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}
\end{align}$

Sandy bought $\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters of blueberries.

###Lindsay

Lindsay bought $2$ cartons with $\dfrac94$ liter of blueberries in each carton.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac94}$ to figure out how many liters of blueberries Lindsay bought:

$\begin{align}
&\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac94}\\\\
=&\dfrac{\maroonD{2 \times}\maroonD9} {\maroonD4}\\\\
=&\maroonD{\dfrac{18}{4}}
\end{align}$

Sandy |  | Lindsay
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters | $=$ | $\maroonD{\dfrac{18}{4}}$ liters
They bought the same number of liters of blueberries.

choices.content: They bought the same number of liters of blueberries.

items.question_content:
Last week, Becky went to dance class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ hours.

This week, Becky went to dance class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ hour.

**Which week did Becky spend more total time in dance class?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Last week

Last week, Becky went to class $4$ times. Each class was $\dfrac32$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class last week: 

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{4 \times \dfrac32}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{4 \times}\blueD 3} {\blueD2}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{12}{2}}\\\\
&=\blueD{6}
\end{align}$

Becky went to class for $\blueD{6}$ hours last week.

###This week

This week, Becky went to class $5$ times. Each class was $1$ an hour long.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{5 \times 1}$ to figure out the  time Becky spent in dance class this week: 

$\maroonD5\times\maroonD1=\maroonD5$

Becky went to class for $\maroonD{5}$ hours this week.

Last week |  | This week
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD{6}$ hours | $>$ | $\maroonD{5}$ hours
Becky spent more total time in dance class last week.

choices.content: She spent the same amount of time in dance class each week.

items.question_content:
Raul bought $6$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac23$ of them.

Omar bought $8$ tangerines and ate $\dfrac14$ of them.

**Who ate more tangerines?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Eating a fraction of the tangerines is the same as eating that fraction times the number of tangerines each person had.

###Raul

Raul ate $\blueD{\dfrac23 \text{ of }6}$ tangerines.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{\dfrac23\times6}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Raul ate: 

$\begin{align}
\blueD{\dfrac23\text{ of }6} &=~ \blueD{\dfrac23 \times 6}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{2\times6}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac{12}{3}}\\\\
&= \blueD4
\end{align}$

Raul ate $\blueD4$ tangerines.

###Omar 

Omar ate $\maroonD{\dfrac14 \text{ of }8}$ tangerines.



[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{\dfrac14\times8}$ to figure out the  amount of tangerines Omar ate:

$\begin{align}
\maroonD{\dfrac14\text{ of }8} ~&=~ \maroonD{\dfrac14 \times 8}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{1\times8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{4}}\\\\
&= \maroonD2
\end{align}$

Omar ate $\maroonD2$ tangerines.

Raul |  | Omar
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\blueD4$ tangerines | $>$ | $\maroonD2$ tangerines
Raul ate more tangerines.

choices.content: They ate the same number of tangerines.

items.question_content:
Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

**Who ran more kilometers in total?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Tess

Tess ran $\dfrac43 $ kilometers each day for $2$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac43}$ to figure out the total distance Tess ran:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\maroonD{2 \times \dfrac43}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\maroonD{2 \times}\maroonD4} {\maroonD3}\\\\
&=\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}}
\end{align}$

Tess ran $\maroonD{\dfrac83}$ kilometers.

###Cam

Cam ran $\dfrac23 $ of a kilometer each day for $5$ days.

[[☃ image 1]]

We can multiply $\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}$ to figure out the total distance Cam ran:

$\begin{align}
&\quad\blueD{5 \times \dfrac23}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\blueD{5 \times}\blueD2} {\blueD3}\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}}
\end{align}$

Cam ran $\blueD{\dfrac{10}3}$ kilometers.

Tess |  | Cam
:-: | :-: | :-:
$\maroonD{\dfrac{8}{3}} \text{ km}$ | $<$ | $\blueD{\dfrac{10}{3}} \text{ km}$
Cam ran more kilometers in total.

choices.content: They ran the same number of total kilometers.

items.question_content:
The pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits on it as the tomato plant has. The tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it. 

**How many fruits does the pepper plant have on it?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] fruits
hints:
A tomato plant has $9$ fruits on it.

![9 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/25f5c8fbc09be9480b3c740ba974dae49fdfe9be)

The pepper plant has $\dfrac23$ as many fruits as the tomato plant.

Let's break the $9$ tomatoes into $3$ equal groups.

![The same 9 circles in 3 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3c6172f2db0c563ee435bfa4cab1f0b0cfb48b21)

The pepper plant has $2$ of the $3$ equal groups.

![6 circles in 2 groups of 3.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a7b84b16f38612f53c8a4b0a69b9d739ac0e58c5)

There are $6$ peppers.

Since the pepper plant has $\dfrac{2}{3}$ as many fruits, we can also multiply $9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}$ to find how many fruits are on each plant.

$\begin{align}
&9 \times \dfrac{2}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{9 \times 2} {3}\\\\
=&\dfrac{18}{3}\\\\
=&\dfrac61\\\\
=&{6}
\end{align}$
The pepper plant has $6$ fruits on it.

**How many chew toys does Annie's dog have?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] chew toys
hints:
Sophie's dog has $8$ chew toys.

![8 circles.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/554b2f377f347babd2228e9e733f109283df82ce)

Annie's dog has $\dfrac14$ as many chew toys as Sophie's dog.

Let's break the $8$ chew toys into $4$ equal groups.

![The same 8 circles in 4 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e9ae586963c5d9533193905a306bcf45de9b58a2)

Annie's dog has $1$ of the $4$ equal groups.

![2 circles in 1 group of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9d419f1ebfed90f4a0efed58c146fc19f941cb5f)

There are $2$ chew toys.

Since Annie's dog has $\dfrac{1}{4}$ as many chew toys, we can also multiply $8 \times \dfrac{1}{4}$ to find how many chew toys she has.

$\begin{align}
&8 \times \dfrac{1}{4}\\\\
=&\dfrac{8 \times 1} {4}\\\\
=&\dfrac{8}{4}\\\\
=&{2}
\end{align}$
Annie's dog has $2$ chew toys.

items.question_content:
Mark and Damien were in a hot dog eating contest. Mark ate $12$ hot dogs. Damien ate $\dfrac46$ as many hot dogs as Mark.

**How many hot dogs did Damien eat?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] hot dogs
hints:
Mark ate $12$ hot dogs.


[[☃ image 1]]

Damien ate $\dfrac46$ as many hot dogs as Mark..

Let's break the $12$ hot dogs into $6$ equal groups.

![The same 12 hot dogs in 6 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)

Damien ate $4$ of the $6$ equal groups.

![8 hot dogs in 4 groups of 2.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c) ![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/41ac4ceec7a281b453240fdc2fb5747243efbf9c)

There are $8$ hot dogs.

Since Damien ate $\dfrac{4}{6}$ as many hot dogs, we can also multiply $12 \times \dfrac{4}{6}$ to find how many hot dogs he ate.

$\begin{align}
&12 \times \dfrac{4}{6}\\\\
=&\dfrac{12 \times 4} {6}\\\\
=&\dfrac{48}{6}\\\\
=&{8}
\end{align}$
Damien ate $8$ hot dogs.
